title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling the Dracut FIPS module requires modifying kernel boot parameters and enabling FIPS mode, which is not supported on an already installed system and can cause system lockout or break critical services. Policy classifies FIPS changes as too dangerous to remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter change; potential system lockout; unsupported on existing installations

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot argument and updating the system policy, which cannot be applied to an already installed system without a reinstall. Changing boot parameters can render the system unbootable or lock out access if not performed correctly, so remediation is considered too dangerous.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader; kernel_params; FIPS; system_lockout

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips=1 kernel boot parameter, which is only supported at installation time. Changing it on an existing system is not supported and may render the system unbootable or cause service disruption. This constitutes a high‑risk bootloader/kernel change.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; FIPS mode activation; kernel boot parameter modification; potential system instability or unbootability

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Remediating this finding requires upgrading or replacing the operating system to a vendor-supported version, which can cause service disruption, require a reboot, and may introduce compatibility issues. This is a significant change that should be planned and reviewed by an administrator.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; potential loss of SSH/console access during upgrade; service downtime; compatibility issues with existing applications

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Uninstall gssproxy Package
triage_reason: Removing gssproxy can break services that depend on it for authentication (e.g., Kerberos, SSSD, SSH GSSAPI). This poses a risk of losing authentication capability or causing service disruption, so a human should evaluate dependencies before removal.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; authentication disruption; dependency breakage; potential service outage

title: Ensure Red Hat GPG Key Installed
triage_reason: The remediation only imports a trusted GPG key and verifies directory permissions, which is a standard, low‑impact operation that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; missing_gpg_key
attempt_1_commands:
ls -ld /media/cdrom; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey* --qf '%{NAME}-%{VERSION}-%{RELEASE} %{SIGPG}
'; chmod 755 /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; curl -s -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release https://www.redhat.com/security/fd/keys/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm --import /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm --import https://www.redhat.com/security/fd/keys/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; curl -s -o /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release https://ftp.redhat.com/pub/redhat/linux/enterprise/9Server/en/os/x86_64/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
attempt_3_commands: dnf install -y redhat-release; rpm -q --filesbypkg redhat-release; dnf install -y redhat-release; wget -q -O /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release https://www.redhat.com/security/fd/keys/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; ls -l /etc/pki/rpm-gpg; gpg --with-fingerprint /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; dnf install -y gnupg; gpg --with-fingerprint /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; gpg2 --with-fingerprint /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release; rpm -qa gpg-pubkey*; rpm -qi gpg-pubkey-350d275d-6279464b; gpg --show-keys /etc/pki/rpm-gpg/RPM-GPG-KEY-redhat-release
review_feedback: The plan now includes fingerprint verification, installs the missing package, corrects directory and file permissions, and ensures the key is imported. It addresses the concerns from the previous review.
review_concerns: Key file permissions are set to 644, which allows world‑readable access; 600 would be stricter.; The plan assumes gpg is available and does not explicitly check for its presence.; No explicit error handling is described for cases where the fingerprint does not match or the import fails.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only checks for the presence of the Red Hat GPG key, installs the providing package if missing, verifies the fingerprint, corrects directory and file permissions, and imports the key if needed. These actions are standard and do not interfere with critical services or authentication mechanisms. Permissions changes are permissive but not destructive, and installing the redhat-release package on Rocky Linux is safe though may be a no‑op if the package is already present.
qa_side_effects: Potential installation of the redhat-release package, which may add or update files but does not affect service operation.; Key file becomes world‑readable (mode 644), slightly reducing confidentiality but not impacting functionality.

title: Modify the System Login Banner
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/issue, a pre‑login banner file. This change does not affect authentication mechanisms, network services, or critical system components, and can be validated quickly without risking lockout.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected
attempt_3_commands: cp /etc/issue /etc/issue.bak; install -m 0644 -o root -g root /etc/issue /etc/issue; ls -l /etc/issue; cat /etc/issue
review_feedback: The plan addresses the vulnerability by backing up /etc/issue, writing the required DoD banner, and resetting permissions. It meets the primary remediation goals, though a few minor enhancements could improve robustness.
review_concerns: The plan assumes the write_file operation will correctly preserve line breaks and add a trailing newline; this is not explicitly verified.; SELinux file context is not addressed, which could affect the banner display on systems with enforcing policies.; Verification only displays the file; a diff against the expected content would provide stronger assurance.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies /etc/issue, a non-critical display file, with a backup, correct permissions, and verification. It does not affect SSH authentication, system services, or networking. The changes are straightforward and unlikely to cause outages.
qa_side_effects: Potential SELinux context reset on /etc/issue if install does not preserve the original label

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Digit Characters
triage_reason: The finding requires a simple PAM configuration edit to enforce a minimum digit requirement. This follows standard STIG hardening patterns, does not alter system boot or critical services, and poses minimal risk of lockout if tested before deployment.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; password_policy_enforcement; potential_user_lockout
review_feedback: The plan will set dcredit to -1, which enforces at least one digit in passwords and resolves the vulnerability. It correctly handles existing lines and appends the setting if missing. However, it could be improved with backups, permission enforcement, and handling of pwquality.conf.d files.
review_concerns: No backup of the original configuration file is made before editing.; File permissions are assumed unchanged but not explicitly enforced after the append operation.; The plan only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf and may miss overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting dcredit=-1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf enforces a digit requirement for new passwords without altering PAM module loading or other services. The change is limited to password quality enforcement and will not prevent existing logins or break SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The only impact is that password changes that do not include a digit will be rejected, which is expected behavior for this remediation.
qa_side_effects: Password change operations may fail if the new password lacks a digit.; Users may need to update scripts or automation that set passwords without digits.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Enforce for root User
triage_reason: The remediation only adds the enforce_for_root directive to /etc/security/pwquality.conf, a standard PAM password quality configuration. This change does not modify services, kernel parameters, or boot settings, and is unlikely to cause lockout or system instability. It follows typical STIG hardening patterns for authentication.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected
attempt_2_commands: touch /etc/security/pwquality.conf; cp -a /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S).bak; sed -i 's/^\s*#\?enforce_for_root.*/enforce_for_root = 1/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/enforce_for_root/!$a enforce_for_root = 1' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -q 'enforce_for_root' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
review_feedback: The plan will correctly add or update enforce_for_root = 1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf while creating a timestamped backup, addressing the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The abstract read_file/write_file steps may not preserve SELinux context or extended attributes when overwriting the file.; If the file does not exist, the backup will be of an empty file; creating the backup after touching may result in an empty backup.; The plan does not explicitly use an atomic replace (e.g., mv -Z) which could briefly leave the file in an inconsistent state.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly adds or updates the enforce_for_root = 1 setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf, preserving permissions and creating a backup. This change only affects password complexity enforcement for the root account and does not interfere with SSH, PAM authentication, or other critical services. The risk of breaking services is minimal, and any SELinux context loss can be restored from the backup.
qa_side_effects: Possible loss of SELinux context or extended attributes if write_file does not preserve them; Brief moment where the file may be missing or partially written during the replace operation

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Lowercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits the PAM pwquality configuration to require at least one lowercase character. This is a standard STIG hardening change, involves a single file edit, and does not affect bootloader, kernel, or critical services. The risk of accidental lockout is low if the change is applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential_user_lockout
review_feedback: The plan will set lcredit to -1 and thus satisfy the requirement to enforce at least one lowercase character. However, it could be strengthened by creating a backup, handling possible overrides in pwquality.conf.d, and using a more robust regex.
review_concerns: Only edits /etc/security/pwquality.conf and may miss overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf; Does not create a backup before modifying the file; Sed regex may fail to match lines with leading whitespace before the comment character; Does not verify or enforce correct file permissions after edit
qa_verdict_reason: Setting lcredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf enforces at least one lowercase character in new passwords, which is the intended remediation. The change only affects password complexity checks and does not alter PAM module loading or other system services, so it is unlikely to cause service outages or lock out existing users. The regex used is straightforward and will correctly replace or add the setting.
qa_side_effects: Password change operations will require at least one lowercase character, potentially causing password that do not meet the new requirement to be rejected.; If the file syntax is corrupted (e.g., by an incorrect sed replacement), PAM password checks could fail, but the provided command is simple and safe.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Length
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies the pam_pwquality configuration to set a minimum password length. This is a standard STIG hardening step with minimal risk of causing lockout or service disruption, and it does not affect bootloader, filesystem, or networking.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential user lockout if length is set too high; misconfiguration could affect authentication
attempt_2_commands: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*#\?\s*minlen[[:space:]]*=.*/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'minlen = 8' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -R '^[[:space:]]*minlen' /etc/security/pwquality.conf* | wc -l; sed -i '/minlen/d' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'minlen = 8' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf; grep -R 'minlen' /etc/security/pwquality.conf* | wc -l; grep -E '^[[:space:]]*minlen' /etc/security/pwquality.conf | wc -l; grep -E '^[[:space:]]*minlen' /etc/security/pwquality.conf | awk -F= '{print $2}' | tr -d ' '; ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d 2>/dev/null || true
attempt_3_commands: ls -1 /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; stat -c '%F %N' /etc/security/pwquality.conf; authselect current-profile; authselect current; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.bak-$(date +%s); authselect create-profile pwlen_hardening -b custom/hardening; mkdir -p /etc/authselect/custom/pwlen_hardening/etc/security; cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/authselect/custom/pwlen_hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf; sed -i '/^\s*#\?\s*minlen\s*=.*$/d' /etc/authselect/custom/pwlen_hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf; echo 'minlen = 8' >> /etc/authselect/custom/pwlen_hardening/etc/security/pwquality.conf; authselect select pwlen_hardening --force; authselect select custom/pwlen_hardening --force
review_feedback: The plan uses authselect to create a custom profile and sets minlen=8 in the profile's pwquality.conf, which will enforce the required minimum password length. It includes backups and verification steps, but it does not address possible overriding settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, does not use an atomic edit method, and does not restart services to apply the change immediately.
review_concerns: Existing minlen entries in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf could override the setting applied in the custom profile.; The sed command may not match all comment variations or whitespace patterns.; Appending the setting without ensuring a trailing newline could corrupt the file format.; No explicit service restart or PAM cache reload is performed, which may delay enforcement until the next login.; Backups are created only for the original pwquality.conf, not for the profile file being edited.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan uses authselect to create a custom profile and set minlen=8, which is the standard RHEL method for PAM configuration changes. It backs up the original file, edits only the custom profile's pwquality.conf, and applies the profile with --force. This should not disrupt critical services or cause immediate lockouts; users with passwords shorter than 8 characters will be prompted to change them on next password change, which is acceptable. The lack of a service restart is not an issue because PAM reads the configuration on each authentication.
qa_side_effects: Users with existing passwords shorter than 8 characters will be required to change them on next password change.; If there are overriding settings in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, the desired minlen may not be applied.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Special Characters
triage_reason: Changing the ocredit setting in /etc/security/pwquality.conf is a standard PAM password policy adjustment that does not affect system boot or critical services and is unlikely to lock out users if performed correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; authentication; password_policy
review_feedback: The plan correctly sets ocredit to -1, which enforces at least one special character. However, it could be improved by adding a backup of the configuration file, verifying that the pam_pwquality module is present, and checking for existing ocredit settings in any pwquality.conf.d/*.conf files to avoid duplicate entries.
review_concerns: No backup of /etc/security/pwquality.conf before modification.; Potential duplicate ocredit entries if a .d file already defines the parameter.; Does not verify that pam_pwquality (libpwquality) is installed and active.; No check for file permissions or ownership after edit.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly enforces a minimum of one special character by setting ocredit = -1 in /etc/security/pwquality.conf. This change only tightens password complexity and does not affect system boot or critical services. The sed and echo commands are straightforward and unlikely to introduce syntax errors. While a backup and duplicate‑entry check would be prudent, the remediation itself is unlikely to cause lockouts or service outages.
qa_side_effects: Existing users may be unable to change passwords that do not contain a special character.; If a pwquality.conf.d/*.conf file also defines ocredit, duplicate settings could cause ambiguous behavior.; No backup is made before editing, so a malformed file could affect password quality checks.

title: Ensure PAM Enforces Password Requirements - Minimum Uppercase Characters
triage_reason: The remediation only edits a PAM password quality configuration file to require at least one uppercase character. This is a standard STIG hardening change, does not affect system boot or critical services, and can be applied without risking loss of console or SSH access if performed correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; password_policy; potential_user_lockout
review_feedback: The plan will set ucredit to -1 in pwquality.conf, which addresses the requirement, but it could be more robust and comprehensive.
review_concerns: Does not handle possible overrides in /etc/security/pwquality.conf.d/*.conf, which could negate the setting.; The sed regex may miss lines with leading whitespace or inline comments, leaving the old value unchanged.; No backup of the original configuration file is made before modification.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only modifies the pwquality configuration to require at least one uppercase character. This does not affect authentication flow for existing sessions and is unlikely to cause service outages. The change may be ineffective if overridden by .conf.d files or if whitespace is present, but it will not break critical services.
qa_side_effects: Users changing passwords must include an uppercase character, which may cause password change failures if they are unaware of the new requirement.; Existing passwords remain unchanged, so no immediate lockout occurs.

title: Configure Logind to terminate idle sessions after certain time of inactivity
triage_reason: The remediation only requires editing a single systemd configuration file to set StopIdleSessionSec= and restarting the logind service. This is a standard STIG hardening step with minimal risk of lockout or service disruption.
triage_estimated_impact: service restart may temporarily affect active sessions
attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/systemd/logind.conf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak; sed -i 's/^#\?\s*StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=600/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/^\[Login\]/ { /StopIdleSessionSec=/! a StopIdleSessionSec=600 }' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; sed -i '/^\[Login\]/a StopIdleSessionSec=600' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; systemctl restart systemd-logind
attempt_3_commands: tar -cf /etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak.tar /etc/systemd/logind.conf; dd if=/etc/systemd/logind.conf of=/etc/systemd/logind.conf.bak bs=1M status=none; perl -pi -e 's/^\#?\s*StopIdleSessionSec=.*/StopIdleSessionSec=600/' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; grep -q '^StopIdleSessionSec=' /etc/systemd/logind.conf; systemctl try-reload-or-restart systemd-logind
review_feedback: The plan will correctly set StopIdleSessionSec=600 under the [Login] section and restart the logind service, which resolves the vulnerability. Minor improvements can be made for robustness and adherence to best practices.
review_concerns: Backup is created as a tar archive rather than a direct copy, which may be less convenient for restoration.; The sed insertion will add the setting after every [Login] header if multiple sections exist, potentially creating duplicates.; File permissions and SELinux context are not explicitly preserved after editing.; Using systemctl restart may cause a brief service interruption; reload could be sufficient.; No explicit check for existing un-commented setting before replacement, though perl handles it.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly edits /etc/systemd/logind.conf to set StopIdleSessionSec=600, backs up the file, and restarts systemd-logind. Restarting logind is safe and does not affect core services such as sshd, firewalld, or networking. The changes are limited to logind configuration and are unlikely to cause lock‑outs or data loss.
qa_side_effects: If the file contains multiple [Login] sections, the sed insertion could create duplicate StopIdleSessionSec entries.; Editing the file with perl -i may reset SELinux file context if not preserved.; A brief interruption of the systemd-logind service occurs during restart, which could affect active user sessions but not critical daemons.

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Removing unauthorized local user accounts can inadvertently delete the account currently used for SSH or console access, leading to lockout. A human must verify the authorized list and ensure the current login account is not removed before remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential loss of SSH access; accidental deletion of active user account

title: Ensure All User Initialization Files Have Mode 0740 Or Less Permissive
triage_reason: The remediation only changes file permissions to 0740, which is a standard STIG hardening step and does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or critical services. The change is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; file_permission_change
review_feedback: The proposed find command does not address files that have the group execute bit set, which is disallowed by the required 0740 mode. Using -perm /027 omits the group execute (010) bit, so such files will remain overly permissive. Additionally, the script lacks an explicit root‑privilege check and may affect non‑initialization dotfiles.
review_concerns: Group execute permission (010) is not matched, so files with that bit set remain non‑compliant.; No explicit verification that the command is run as root, which could cause silent failures.; The -maxdepth 2 limit could miss init files stored deeper (e.g., in sub‑directories like .config).; All dotfiles are targeted, including non‑initialization files (e.g., .gitconfig), which may be unnecessary.

title: Ensure the Default Umask is Set Correctly in /etc/profile
triage_reason: The remediation only edits /etc/profile and files in /etc/profile.d to set a default umask. This is a straightforward text substitution that does not affect authentication mechanisms, networking, or boot configuration, and it does not risk locking out users.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; Incorrect default file permissions if umask is set too permissive; Potential for insecure file creation if not corrected
attempt_2_commands: find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \) -print; bash -c 'var_accounts_user_umask=027; for file in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do cp "$file" "$file.bak"; if grep -qE "^\s*umask\s+[0-7]{2,3}" "$file"; then sed -i -E "s/^\s*umask\s+[0-7]{2,3}/umask $var_accounts_user_umask/" "$file"; else echo "umask $var_accounts_user_umask" >> "$file"; fi; done'
attempt_3_commands: find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \) -print; bash -c 'ts=$(date +%s); for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do cp "$f" "$f.bak.$ts"; sed -i -E "/^\s*umask\s+[0-7]{2,3}/c\\umask 027; t; $a\\umask 027" "$f"; done'; for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do echo -n "$f: "; grep -E "^\s*umask" "$f" | wc -l; done; bash -c 'ts=$(date +%s); for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do cp "$f" "$f.bak.$ts"; sed -i -E "/^[^#]*\s*umask\s+[0-7]{2,3}/d" "$f"; echo "umask 027" >> "$f"; done'; for f in /etc/profile $(find /etc/profile.d -type f \( -name "*.sh" -o -name "sh.local" \)); do echo -n "$f: "; grep -E "^[^#]*\s*umask" "$f" | wc -l; done
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up files, ensures a single umask line set to 027, and verifies the result, which will remediate the vulnerability. Minor improvements to the regex and handling of edge cases would make it more robust.
review_concerns: The grep regex '^[\s*umask' does not exclude commented lines, potentially counting or modifying commented umask statements.; The plan does not explicitly handle two‑digit umask values (e.g., 022) which may be missed by a stricter numeric regex.; If a file contains multiple umask lines, the sed replacement must ensure all existing lines are removed before appending a single line; the plan does not detail this.; Potential edge case: files that already contain the correct line may still be rewritten unnecessarily if the count check is not precise.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly backs up each profile file, ensures a single non‑commented umask line set to 027, and validates the change. Setting a default umask of 027 is a standard hardening measure and does not interfere with critical daemons. The regex used excludes commented lines, and the modifications are limited to /etc/profile and /etc/profile.d scripts, which are only sourced by login shells.
qa_side_effects: User‑created files will inherit a more restrictive permission mask (027) which may affect scripts that expect looser defaults.; If a script explicitly sets its own umask later, the change in /etc/profile will be overridden, but this is benign.

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: Changing the GRUB2 superuser username modifies bootloader configuration. Misconfiguration can prevent the system from booting or lock out console access, which is considered a high‑risk bootloader change per policy.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; bootloader misconfiguration; potential boot failure; loss of console access

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Setting a GRUB2 boot loader password modifies the bootloader configuration, which can lock out access if misconfigured or forgotten. According to policy, bootloader changes are classified as too_dangerous_to_remediate.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; bootloader_lockout; system_unbootable; password_forget

title: Configure Multiple DNS Servers in /etc/resolv.conf
triage_reason: The remediation involves editing /etc/resolv.conf to add additional nameserver entries, which is a straightforward file modification with minimal risk of locking out users or breaking critical services.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; Incorrect DNS entries could cause temporary name resolution failures
attempt_1_commands: grep -q '^nameserver 8\.8\.8\.8' /etc/resolv.conf && echo found || echo notfound; sed -i '$ a nameserver 8.8.8.8' /etc/resolv.conf
review_feedback: The plan will add a second DNS server when needed, satisfying the requirement in most cases. However, it may not handle the case where no nameserver entries exist and does not verify that DNS resolution is enabled before modifying the file.
review_concerns: If /etc/resolv.conf contains zero nameserver entries, the plan adds only one (8.8.8.8), leaving the system with a single DNS server, which does not meet the 'multiple DNS servers' requirement.; The plan adds a public DNS server (8.8.8.8) without considering internal DNS policies or network restrictions.; The plan reads /etc/nsswitch.conf but does not conditionally act based on whether the 'dns' entry is present, potentially modifying resolv.conf unnecessarily.; No backup of /etc/resolv.conf is made before editing, which could risk loss of original configuration if the command fails.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan only appends a public IPv4 nameserver to /etc/resolv.conf when fewer than two entries exist. This is a low‑impact change that does not modify critical system files or services, and is unlikely to cause SSH lockout, authentication failures, or service outages. While it may not fully satisfy the multiple‑DNS requirement in the zero‑entry case and could conflict with internal DNS policies, it does not pose a serious risk to system stability.
qa_side_effects: Adds a public DNS server (8.8.8.8) which may be undesirable in restricted environments; If /etc/resolv.conf is managed by NetworkManager or systemd‑resolved, the manual edit could be overwritten on the next network restart; When the file originally has zero nameserver entries, only one server will be added, leaving the system with a single DNS server; No backup of the original resolv.conf is created, so a failed edit could lose existing custom entries

title: Disable Kernel Parameter for IPv4 Forwarding on all IPv4 Interfaces
triage_reason: The remediation only modifies a sysctl kernel parameter to disable IPv4 forwarding and adds a persistent entry in /etc/sysctl.d. This is a standard STIG hardening action, does not affect authentication, bootloader, or file system layout, and does not require a reboot. The change is low‑risk and can be automated safely.

qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly adds the nosuid option to the /home entry in /etc/fstab, creates a timestamped backup, uses a field‑aware edit, remounts the filesystem, and verifies the change. Adding nosuid is a standard hardening measure and does not affect core system services. The temporary remount may briefly interrupt active user sessions, but it will not lock out SSH or break authentication.
qa_side_effects: User‑installed setuid binaries located in /home will no longer work; Active user processes may experience a brief pause during the remount of /home

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: The remediation involves adding a sysctl configuration line in /etc/sysctl.d and applying it, which is a standard, low‑risk operation. It does not affect authentication, networking, or bootloader settings, and can be applied without a reboot. While it may impact container workloads, it does not risk lockout or critical service failure, aligning with the policy for safe sysctl persistence changes.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; potential_container_breakage; service_disruption
attempt_3_commands: ls -l /etc/sysctl.d/99-user-max-user-namespaces.conf; systemctl restart systemd-sysctl.service; sysctl -n user.max_user_namespaces
review_feedback: The plan will set and persist user.max_user_namespaces=0, which resolves the vulnerability. However it does not address container workload requirements and uses a reload method that may not be portable across all distributions.
review_concerns: May overwrite existing sysctl settings if the target file already contains other entries.; Uses `systemctl restart systemd-sysctl.service`, which may not exist on all Linux distributions; `sysctl --system` is more portable.; Does not include conditional handling or documentation for container environments that may need a non‑zero value.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets user.max_user_namespaces=0 via a sysctl file and reloads it using systemd-sysctl, which does not affect core services like sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The main risk is to container workloads that rely on unprivileged user namespaces and to any other sysctl settings that might be present in the target file.
qa_side_effects: Container runtimes (e.g., Docker, Podman) may fail to start or run unprivileged containers.; If /etc/sysctl.d/99-user-max-user-namespaces.conf already contains other settings, overwriting it will lose those configurations.

title: Restrict Exposed Kernel Pointer Addresses Access
triage_reason: The remediation only involves commenting out existing kernel.kptr_restrict entries in sysctl.d files and adding a persistent setting. This is a standard sysctl configuration change that does not affect authentication, networking, or critical services and does not require a reboot.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; kernel_parameter; information_leak
attempt_1_commands: grep -lP '^(?!#).*kernel.kptr_restrict' /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; sed -i 's/^\s*kernel\.kptr_restrict/# &/' /etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf; sysctl -w kernel.kptr_restrict=2; sysctl kernel.kptr_restrict
attempt_2_commands: sed -i "/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.kptr_restrict/ s/^/# /" /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null; sed -i "/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.kptr_restrict/ s/^/# /" /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf; sysctl --system; sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*kernel\.kptr_restrict[[:space:]]*=/ s/^/# /' /etc/sysctl.conf; sysctl --system; sysctl kernel.kptr_restrict
review_feedback: The plan will comment out existing kernel.kptr_restrict entries, add a persistent configuration with the secure value 2, and reload sysctl settings, which should remediate the vulnerability.
review_concerns: The sed command does not explicitly handle lines that contain spaces around the '=' sign or inline comments, though it will still comment the whole line.; The description of using "write_file with mode 0644" is vague; the actual command to create the file should be explicit to avoid permission issues.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly comments out any existing kernel.kptr_restrict entries, adds a persistent configuration setting the value to 2, and reloads sysctl. Changing kernel.kptr_restrict to 2 is a standard hardening measure and does not affect core services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The commands are safe, permissions are appropriate, and no disruptive actions are performed.

title: Enable Randomized Layout of Virtual Address Space
triage_reason: Enabling kernel.randomize_va_space is a standard sysctl hardening step that can be applied at runtime and persisted without affecting authentication, networking, or filesystem layout. The change is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.

review_feedback: The plan correctly sets the runtime value and creates a dedicated persistent sysctl configuration, addressing the vulnerability. It avoids bulk editing and ensures proper permissions. Minor improvements around atomic file creation, SELinux context handling, and precise reloading can be made.
review_concerns: The new config file is written directly without an atomic rename, which could leave a partially written file on failure.; SELinux context for the new file is not explicitly set, which may cause the file to be ignored on SELinux‑enforced systems.; The plan uses sysctl --system to reload all settings; using sysctl -p on the specific file would be more targeted.; Potential race condition if multiple processes modify /proc/sys simultaneously, though unlikely.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly sets kernel.randomize_va_space to the recommended value (2) both at runtime and persistently. The change is limited to a kernel security parameter that does not affect core services such as SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The steps avoid bulk edits, use proper permissions, and reload the configuration safely.

title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing

title: Uninstall vsftpd Package

attempt_1_commands: rpm -q chrony; systemctl unmask chronyd.service; systemctl enable chronyd.service; systemctl is-system-running; systemctl start chronyd.service; systemctl status chronyd.service; systemctl start chronyd.service; systemctl status chronyd.service

title: Configure Time Service Maxpoll Interval
triage_reason: The remediation only edits NTP/Chrony configuration files to add or modify the maxpoll parameter, a straightforward text substitution. This does not affect authentication, bootloader, or critical system services beyond the time synchronization daemon, and can be safely applied with a service restart if needed.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; filesystems affected; configuration change; potential service restart
attempt_1_commands: rpm -q chrony; rpm -q ntp; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E 's/^([[:space:]]*(server|pool|peer)[^#]*)(maxpoll[[:space:]]+[0-9]+)?(.*)$/\1 maxpoll 10\4/' /etc/chrony.conf; ls -1 /etc/chrony.d/*.conf 2>/dev/null || true; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the time service, edits the appropriate configuration files to enforce a maxpoll of 10, and restarts the service, which should remediate the finding.
review_concerns: The sed command is not fully specified; an incorrect regex could fail to add maxpoll to lines lacking it.; No backup of configuration files is made before in‑place editing, risking loss of original settings if something goes wrong.; The plan does not explicitly handle the case where both chrony and ntpd are installed simultaneously.; Service restart may cause a brief loss of time synchronization; a graceful reload could be preferable.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan correctly identifies the installed time service, edits the relevant configuration files to enforce a maxpoll of 10, and restarts only that service. This is unlikely to affect critical services such as sshd, auditd, or networking. The only risk is a brief loss of time synchronization during the service restart or a malformed config if the sed regex fails, but these are low‑impact and can be mitigated with a backup.
qa_side_effects: Brief interruption of time synchronization while the time service restarts; Potential loss of original configuration if the sed edit fails (no backup); If both chrony and ntpd are installed, the script may modify only one, leaving the other unchanged

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The fix only requires editing the chronyd configuration file to remove non‑server time sources and restarting the service, which is a low‑impact operation with no risk of locking out access.

attempt_1_commands: cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E '/^[[:space:]]*(pool|peer|fallback)[[:space:]]+/ s/^/#/' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd; systemctl is-active chronyd
attempt_3_commands: chronyd -t; chronyd -p; systemctl reload chronyd; systemctl restart chronyd; systemctl is-active chronyd; cp /etc/chrony.conf /etc/chrony.conf.bak; sed -i -E '/^[[:space:]]*(pool|peer|fallback)[[:space:]]+/ s/^/#/' /etc/chrony.conf; chronyd -p; systemctl reload chronyd; systemctl restart chronyd
review_feedback: The plan correctly backs up the configuration, removes non‑server directives, validates the presence of a server entry, and reloads/restarts chronyd. It addresses the vulnerability, though minor refinements could improve safety.
review_concerns: The generic sed -i approach may unintentionally comment out commented lines that contain the words pool/peer/fallback, removing useful documentation.; Using chronyd -p for validation does not catch syntax errors; a dedicated syntax check (chronyd -q or chronyd -t) would be safer.; The plan does not explicitly verify that at least one server directive remains after editing, which could leave chronyd without a time source.

It does not affect SSH, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The main risk is an accidental removal of all server entries or a syntax error that would prevent chronyd from starting, which could disrupt time synchronization but would not lock out access. The approach is appropriate for the vulnerability.

title: Uninstall tftp-server Package

review_feedback: The plan correctly checks for the package, removes it with dnf, and verifies removal. It addresses the OpenSCAP finding and minimizes impact by using --noautoremove.
review_concerns: Potential dependent packages may be left broken if they rely on tftp-server.
qa_verdict_reason: Removing the tftp-server package does not affect core system services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking. The plan checks for installation before removal and verifies the removal after. Using --noautoremove prevents automatic removal of other packages, minimizing impact. The main risk is that any optional packages that depend on tftp-server may become broken, but these are unlikely to be critical for most systems.

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: The finding requires adding a certmap stanza to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf and restarting the SSSD service. This is a standard authentication configuration change with minimal risk of system breakage or lockout when performed correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; potential authentication misconfiguration
attempt_3_commands: cp /etc/sssd/sssd.conf /etc/sssd/sssd.conf.bak; sed -i '/^\[pam\]/a\\[domain/testing.test]\\nid_provider = files' /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; sed -i '/^\[domain\/testing\.test\]/a\\[certmap/testing.test/rule_name]\\nmatchrule = <SAN>.*EDIPI@mil\\nmaprule = (userCertificate;binary={cert!bin})\\ndomains = testing.test' /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; sssd -t; sssctl config-check; systemctl reload sssd; systemctl status sssd; sed -i '1i[sssd]\nndomains = testing.test' /etc/sssd/sssd.conf; systemctl reload sssd; systemctl start sssd; systemctl status sssd
review_feedback: The plan addresses the core requirement by adding the certmap stanza, validating the configuration, and reloading SSSD safely. It includes a backup and status check, which mitigates most risk. Minor improvements such as duplicate detection, permission verification, and a post‑reload authentication test would make it more robust.
review_concerns: The sed command may fail if the domain header contains whitespace or different capitalization.; No check is performed to avoid inserting a duplicate certmap stanza if it already exists.; File ownership and permissions for /etc/sssd/sssd.conf are not verified after editing.; No functional test (e.g., login) is performed after reload to confirm the change works.
qa_verdict_reason: The plan follows a safe workflow: backup, syntax validation, and a reload rather than a full restart. If the new stanza is malformed, sssd -t will abort the change, preventing a broken SSSD service. The changes affect only SSSD configuration and are unlikely to lock out SSH or other critical services.
qa_side_effects: Possible duplicate certmap stanza if it already exists; If the domain header differs in case or whitespace, the sed insertion may not occur; Temporary authentication delays during the reload; File ownership/permissions of sssd.conf not re‑verified after edit

title: System Audit Logs Must Have Mode 0640 or Less Permissive
triage_reason: Changing the audit log file permissions to 0600 is a standard, low‑impact configuration change that does not affect system boot, SSH access, or critical services. It aligns with STIG guidance for audit log protection and is reversible.
triage_estimated_impact: filesystems affected; audit_log_file_permission
review_feedback: The plan correctly identifies the audit log file path and applies a restrictive permission mode, which will address the vulnerability. Minor improvements can make the implementation more robust.
review_concerns: Parsing of auditd.conf may fail if the log_file line is commented out or contains extra whitespace.; The plan does not explicitly check that the target file exists before attempting chmod, which could cause an error.; No sudo is mentioned in the command execution, which may be required for modifying /var/log/audit files.; No verification step after chmod to confirm the permissions were applied.
qa_verdict_reason: Setting the audit log file permissions to 0600 (or 0640) is the recommended remediation and does not interfere with auditd's ability to write or read the log as root. It does not affect other services such as sshd, firewalld, or networking. The operation is low‑impact and aligns with the OpenSCAP rule.
qa_side_effects: If the resolved file path does not exist, chmod will fail with an error but will not cause service disruption.; Running chmod without sudo may fail for non‑root users, but the remediation is expected to be executed with appropriate privileges.
